False cord, fry, and arytenoid cartilage.
These are the only 3 tissues in the human vocal tract that we can use to safely perform distorted vocal techniques.
Everything else on the list are just sub-variants or styles of FC, Fry, and AC based distortion.
Basically there are 3 ways to safely produce vocal distortion.
Fry screams are produced by the true cords.

False cord screams are produced by vibrating the false cords, and a lesser known style where you can vibrate the arytenoid cartilage to produce distortion(similar to how Jazz singers perform the Jazz Growl).
FC and Fry screams can be performed simultaneously as the true cords and false cords are positioned so closely together in the vocal tract.

Some vocal styles cause people to develop common patterns of pronunciation, even across different languages and dialects.
I have memorized these patterns so I don't have an issue understanding extreme vocal lyrics.
I can even tell what technique (Fry, FC, or AC) a person is using based on how they pronounce vowel sounds.
This is why non-metal listeners cannot understand the words.
It is simply a matter of pattern recognition.

I think that the best terminology to convey extreme vocals is as follows.
1.) start with the vocal tissue used to produce the distortion.
Fry = true cords, FC = false cords.
AC = arytenoid cartilage.

Some people talk about other tissues like the epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds as sources of distortion but these have rarely been demonstrated in endoscopy studies and are not viable in long term practice.
2.) Fry and false cord allow you to perform almost the exact same things.

Extreme screaming singing is a product of its time, just like other musical styles and singing techniques.
It evolves with changing aesthetics and technological advancements (software and hardware).
Researching and documenting extreme singing styles from the post-World War II era to the present is both necessary and meaningful.

It is suggested that research on extreme singing styles consider the political, economic, and cultural levels, as well as the climate and hydrological conditions of the countries where these bands originated.
In my personal research, I've found that styles like grindcore and brutal death metal are more likely to emerge in tropical regions.
However, the conditions under which these styles arose seem quite different from those in the US and Japan.

I've been surrounded by it my whole life.
Both of my parents were very amateur musicians.
My parents were big fans of the classic metal of the late 70's and 80's, all the way until the early 2000's.
My eldest brother was very into the late 90's early 2000's Death/metalcore, as he was the approperiate age to see and actively participate in the Myspace wave of those genres.

What really inspired me was my association with my friends, I wasn't super into extreme music like my brother was until I lied to some soon-to-be friends about my experience and knowledge as a musician.
They they exposed me fully into that world, and that is what got me lost into it.
Death Metal,Deathcore,Metalcore,Doom/Sludge,Progressive/Technical Metal,Nu Metal,Symphonic Metal
The vocals are honestly the most important part to me.
Nearly all of these genres have people that sound very unique and push what the human voice is capable of.
The next most important thing to me, is how much groove the instrumental has, and how it all builds and flows together.
Hip-hop/rap is because my mom and brothers also enjoy it, and I really enjoy the wordplay and how much structured and layered meaning the words can have.

Guitar and bass for roughly 10 years, drums for 7 years off and on, and vocals for 9 years.
I tend to pick a scale of some sort and build a chord structure using that scale to some arbitrarily chosen rhythm.
I tend to write rhythm guitar and drums simultaneously, typically hand programming the guitar notes on a midi-piano and then using that to teach it to myself on guitar.
Due to lack of equipment I write drums the same way.
the DAW i use is Reaper, and I use various samples/midi controllers for instruments that I do not physically possess.
I tend to use amp sims for any guitar related work.
Throughout middle and high school, i was in concert band so I was taught how to read sheet music, and the basics of theory.
In college I have taken a music production class, and now have a deeper understanding of composition, and recording/mixing/mastering
